response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Listen, I'm eighty-two years old and I've spent my whole life fixing engines here in Bari, so I know when something doesn't sound right. The same instinct works with this fake content online. When I check social media every few weeks to see what my five children and grandchildren are posting, I see all sorts of nonsense floating around, and honestly, it makes my head spin sometimes. You know what I do? I talk to people directly. That's the mechanic's approach to everything, really. If something seems off about a post, especially these wild stories or too-good-to-be-true claims, I pick up the phone and call one of my kids or friends. I ask them what they think. Sometimes they laugh and tell me it's obviously fake, other times we figure it out together. I don't have fancy education, just lower secondary school like my parents before me, but I've learned that human conversation cuts through confusion better than anything else. When you work with your hands all your life, you develop a sense for what's real and what's manufactured poorly. The same goes for information. Does it feel solid or does it wobble? I've also noticed that when I'm feeling particularly low, which happens more these days since my spouse passed, I'm more vulnerable to believing upsetting things I read online. That's when I need to step back completely. I'll go birdwatching along the Adriatic coast or spend time cooking a proper meal instead. Distance gives perspective. My advice is simple and comes from decades of practical work: don't rush to share anything, ask people you trust what they think, and remember that just because something is written down or in a video doesn't make it true. In my workshop, I always double-check my work before declaring an engine fixed. Apply the same standard to what you read online. And frankly, spending less time on these platforms helps too. I only check in a few times monthly and I'm probably better off for it. Life is complicated enough without filling your mind with fabricated stories.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 82-year-old self-employed mechanic with lower secondary education, high extraversion, and moderate openness. They spend minimal time on social media (few times monthly) and experience depression. Their practical, hands-on occupation and age suggest a pragmatic, experience-based approach. The high extraversion means they value human connection and conversation. Their limited formal education but extensive life experience would lead to practical rather than technical solutions. The depression and widowed status add vulnerability awareness. Their hobbies (cooking, birdwatching, travel) suggest healthy offline alternatives. The mechanical background provides a natural metaphor for detecting what's genuine versus faulty.
response_parsed.tone: conversational, direct, pragmatic, warm but straightforward, grounded in practical experience, occasionally world-weary but engaged
